rows.v: Spaghetti, Bucatini, Linguine, Capellini
Best with oil-based, light tomato, or seafood sauces that coat without weighing the strands down.
Spaghetti's ancestor, itriya, was first recorded in 12th-century Arab-ruled Sicily — making dried long pasta one of Italy's oldest culinary technologies, not a later invention.
Tagliatelle, Fettuccine, Pappardelle, Mafaldine
Built for rich, slow-cooked meat ragùs that cling to the wide, flat surface.
According to legend, tagliatelle's dough should be rolled so thin that, held up to a window, you could see Bologna's hilltop basilica through it.
Penne, Rigatoni, Paccheri, Ziti
Hollow centers and ridges trap chunky sauces, baked cheese, and vodka sauce.
The ridges on pasta like rigatoni and penne rigate aren't just decorative — they exist specifically to trap more sauce per bite than a smooth surface ever could.
Fusilli, Cavatappi, Conchiglie, Trofie
Nooks and curves grab pesto, vegetable sauces, and pasta salad dressings.
Fusilli were traditionally shaped by hand-rolling dough around a thin rod or knitting needle, giving each twist a slightly different, handmade character.
Orecchiette, Cavatelli, Tortellini, Ravioli
Simple butter, broth, or light sauces let the pasta (or filling) be the star.
Tortellini were once considered such a luxury that they were reserved for Sunday lunch — a single bowl of 'little pies' represented a special occasion.
Marinara, Arrabbiata, Puttanesca, Amatriciana
Pair with long strands or short tubes — the acidity cuts through pasta's starch.
Tomatoes didn't arrive in Italy until the 1800s — meaning every 'classic' tomato pasta sauce is, in the scope of Italian history, a relatively recent invention.
Ragù alla Bolognese, Ragù Toscano, Ragù Napoletano, Ragù di Cinghiale
Pair with wide ribbons or large tubes that can carry chunks of meat.
Many Italian families cook ragù in huge batches and freeze it in portions, turning what looks like an all-day project into a 20-minute dinner on a weeknight.
Carbonara, Cacio e Pepe, Besciamella, Salsa al Limone
Pair with long strands or ribbons — the silky sauce coats every inch.
Traditional carbonara contains no cream at all — its silky texture comes purely from emulsifying egg yolks and cheese with the starchy heat of the pasta itself.

groups.name: Herb, Oil & Nut Sauces

rows.v: Pesto Genovese, Aglio e Olio, Sugo di Noci, Bagna Cauda
Pair with twists, shells, or long pasta — texture grabs the sauce's solid bits.
Pesto's name comes from 'pestare,' to pound or crush — a reminder that, before blenders, this vivid green sauce was made entirely by hand with a mortar and pestle.

groups.name_es: Salsas De Hierbas Y Frutos Secos
